text:
If theParis Olympicshad a theme, it would be ГўВҖВңcomeback.ГўВҖВқ The sense of normalcy after TokyoГўВҖВҷs largely amended agenda in 2021, devoid of fans and laced with stringent pandemic-era restrictions.Celine DionГўВҖВҷs triumphant return to the stage, as she closed out theopening ceremonywith a glittering and emotional rendition of Edith Piaf's "Hymne ГғВ  lГўВҖВҷamour.ГўВҖВқ Public health and artful performances aside, however, these Olympics marked the culmination of comeback campaigns for some of Team USAГўВҖВҷs most prominent athletes.

Simone Biles, Sunisa Lee, ShaГўВҖВҷCarri Richardson. Two gymnasts and a sprinter, each of whom has held a central role in our domestic success at the Olympics thus far (the U.S. currently leads with the most gold medals won) and in drawing a global audience to their screens. For all that distinguishes them athletically, thereГўВҖВҷs one commonality that Biles, Lee and Richardson share in earnest ГўВҖВ” their redemption tour.

Before they were Olympians, they were Simone, Sunisa and ShaГўВҖВҷCarri.

Currently, each of these women is at the height of their physical performance, further cementing themselves as exemplars of their respective sports through their Olympic efforts. But how do we conceptualize them outside of this mold? Before the aerials, the back handsprings, the 100-meter dashes and relay handoffs, these seemingly otherworldly talents are still regular people. And regular people, as anyone can attest, are saddled with issues outside of their professional lives that still directly affect them. We can look then, to this trio's comeback stories and successes in Paris as a reminder that we should view people holistically, not merely through the lens of their chosen disciplines or what they most excel at.ГӮ

Related

In Salon's recentinterviewfor the Netflix documentary, ГўВҖВңSimone Biles: Rising,ГўВҖВқ director Katie Walsh shared that Biles felt it was pivotal to ГўВҖВңgive people a window into her entire self.ГўВҖВқ

ГўВҖВңWe all see her on the floor as the G.O.A.T. and the greatest ever. But we all don't get to see and have the privilege of getting to know Simone, the human being,ГўВҖВқ Walsh said. ГўВҖВңAnd I know for her, she wants to be looked at and viewed as a full human and not just this amazing gymnast you've come to really expect to be perfect at all times.ГўВҖВқ

Given that Biles, who recently becamethe most decorated American gymnast of all time, is widely regarded as an uncontested talent in the sport, a comeback isn't necessarily something that fans might have expected of her. What would she have to return to if sheГўВҖВҷs already at the top?

WalshГўВҖВҷs sentiments ГўВҖВ” and the documentary more broadly ГўВҖВ” follow a debilitating era in the BilesГўВҖВҷ history in gymnastics. After suffering a bout of theГўВҖВңtwisties,ГўВҖВқГӮВ a disorienting sensation that unmoored BilesГўВҖВҷ body from her mind in Tokyo, she elected to withdraw from the Games. ГўВҖВңI have to focus on my mental health,ГўВҖВқ Biles said at the time,per the BBC.ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt trust myself as much anymore.ГўВҖВқ

Simone Biles Rising (Netflix)Evidently, neither did a number of others. Rather than being lauded for her bravery (and numerous medals from the Rio Games in 2016), her decision was widely panned by online critics who scrutinized her ГўВҖВңabandonmentГўВҖВқ of her teammates. Media mudslinging abounded on various social media platforms. Journalist Tim Constantine swiftly authored anop-ed for The Washington Timestitled, ГўВҖВңSimone Biles is no hero. She is a quitter.ГўВҖВқ Coupled with her 2021testimonybefore the U.S. Senate regarding sexual abuse she and other members of USA Gymnastics endured by former team doctor, Larry Nassar, BilesГўВҖВҷ mental health quickly deteriorated.ГӮ

Three years later, Biles has flipped the script on the haters, outperforming even herself with three gold medals ГўВҖВ” all-around team, all-around individual, and vault ГўВҖВ” and one silver ГўВҖВ” for the floor event ГўВҖВ” to add to her collection. ГўВҖВңBeing in a good mental spot, seeing my therapist every Thursday is kind of religious for me. So thatГўВҖВҷs why IГўВҖВҷm here today,ГўВҖВқ the phenomtold NBCin a June interview, openly attributing consistent mental care to her sparkling return.ГӮ

Many Americans can and should look to BilesГўВҖВҷ embracement of mental health services for inspiration. A 2021reportfrom the Mental Health Million Project found that 45 percent of individuals in the U.S. suffering from a clinical mental problem do not seek help. While the reasons behind why people forgo treatment are varied and often subjective, AmericaГўВҖВҷs cultural dismissal of therapy and general obstinance about prioritizing mental health gives credence to the intense backlash Biles faced post-Tokyo.ГӮ

For Lee, the issues plaguing her physical health found a nexus with her mental state. Her path to the Paris Games was stymied by a diagnosis in 2022. A chronic, incurable kidney disease left her with a litany of adverse symptoms.ГӮ

ГўВҖВңIt wasnГўВҖВҷt something like I can just take a pill and be better; I was going to have to deal with this my whole life,ГўВҖВқ Lee, who earned agold medalin the individual all-around in Tokyo, told the New York Times in aninterviewpublished last month.

While Lee hasnГўВҖВҷt publicly shared the specifics of her condition ГўВҖВңbecause it may change as her medical team learns more about her illness,ГўВҖВқas noted by TODAY,abnormal swelling, hot flashes, cold spells and headaches forced her to modify her training and begin intensive physical therapy. It was an ironic situation in the fullest sense ГўВҖВ” her physical prowess, what her worth had hinged upon as far as society was concerned, was at bitter odds with her body.

ГўВҖВңI was like, who is this person looking back at me?ГўВҖВқ Lee told The Times. ГўВҖВңIt was so scary. I didnГўВҖВҷt know it then, but the old Suni was gone. And she would never be back.ГўВҖВқ

Sunisa Lee of Team United States on Balance Beam competes during the women's Artistic Gymnastics All-Around Final on day six of the Olympic Games Paris 2024 at Bercy Arena on August 01, 2024 in Paris, France. (Stefan Matzke ГўВҖВ“ sampics/Getty Images)

Champions are made, not by pandering to the noise, but by believing in the fullest version of oneself.

LeeГўВҖВҷs case is only one instance of how invisible illnesses remain an unaddressed issue in athletics; oftentimes, interest and sympathy are only assigned to ailments that can be perceived outwardly. In a 2023column for Slate,ГӮВ Fortesa Latifi writes, ГўВҖВңChronic illness forces you to make a choice: look at your body differently, as something that is imperfect, or spend your entire life mourning what has been left behind in the land of health. For chronically ill athletes, it can be a tightrope walk to figure out how to push their bodies while respecting the limits of their diseases.ГўВҖВқ Oftentimes, thereГўВҖВҷs a chasmic dissonance between the urge to fulfill societal expectations of the quintessential athlete and what can actually be accomplished while oneГўВҖВҷs health flounders. As alongtime competitive runnerand chronically ill person, this is an experience I can speak to directly.

Lee however remained undeterred by her illness and summoned the gumption and self-confidence to return to the mat after a brief hiatus, brushing aside the narrowly construed frameworks of athleticism. ГўВҖВңThis comeback was so much more than my return to elite gymnastics,ГўВҖВқ she wrote in a 2023 Instagrampost.ГўВҖВңIt was me proving to myself that I can overcome hard things, and to hopefully inspire others to never let lifeГўВҖВҷs setbacks stop you from going after your dreams.ГўВҖВқ

https://www.instagram.com/p/CvnNv1TO648/?igshid=NTY1MGJiNDNiYw%3D%3D&img_index=1

ГўВҖВңMy doctor was telling me he didnГўВҖВҷt think IГўВҖВҷd be able to do gymnastics ever again,ГўВҖВқ Lee shared with TODAY. ГўВҖВңSo to even be here is an accomplishment in itself. And IГўВҖВҷm super proud of myself.ГўВҖВқ

Then comes Richardson, the sprinting dynamo who saw her first shot at the Olympics dashed after testing positive for marijuana, leading to a 30-day suspension that saw her barred from Tokyo despite winning the 100-meter event at the trials. But to hear it in her own words, it was aГӮВ  ГўВҖВңnerve triggeringГўВҖВқ event that Richardson said sent her spiraling.Speaking to NBCaround the time of her suspension, Richardson claimed she had used drugs to cope with learning of the death of her biological mother. ГўВҖВңIt sent me into a state of emotional panic,ГўВҖВқ she said. ГўВҖВңI didnГўВҖВҷt know how to control my emotions or deal with my emotions during that time.ГўВҖВқ

ShaГўВҖВҷCarri Richardson (@itskerrii) joins us live for an exclusive interview to discuss the positive marijuana test thatГўВҖВҷs put her Olympic future in limbo.pic.twitter.com/iVBp3zhvja

ГўВҖВ” TODAY (@TODAYshow)July 2, 2021



While Richardson did objectively violate rules set in place by U.S.A. Track & Field and International Olympic Committee, her penalty was cast into speculation when the Court of Arbitration for Sports (CAS) decided topermitRussianfigure skater Kamila Valievato compete after she tested positive for trimetazidine, a drug often used to treat heart conditions like angina.

"Can we get a solid answer on the difference of her situation and mines?" Richardsonwrote on a USA Today post,sharing the news that Valieva would be allowed to compete. "My mother died, and I can't run and was also favored to place Top 3. The only difference I see is I'm a Black young lady."

Call it an ambition fueled by being dealt a shady hand, but RichardsonГўВҖВҷs experience seemed to have only emboldened her spirits as she sought to redeem her image. At the 2023 World Championships in Budapest, Richardson bested top Jamaican athletes Shericka Jackson and Shelly-Ann Fraser Pryce in the 100-meter dash, taking the win in a blistering 10.65 seconds. Afterward, she self-assuredly uttered the signature mantra sheГўВҖВҷs proclaimed repeatedly over the past year: ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВҷm not back. IГўВҖВҷm better.ГўВҖВқ

ShaГўВҖВҷCarri Richardson: ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВҷm not back IГўВҖВҷm betterГўВҖВқpic.twitter.com/2iNR9VVeOP

ГўВҖВ” Travis Miller (@travismillerx13)July 8, 2023



Richardson elaborated on the saying in aninterview with Voguepublished last month. ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt just mean IГўВҖВҷm a better runner,ГўВҖВқ she said. ГўВҖВңItГўВҖВҷs beyond that. IГўВҖВҷm better at being ShaГўВҖВҷCarri. IГўВҖВҷm better at being myself.ГўВҖВқ

ItГўВҖВҷs easy to perceive Olympians as athletes. ItГўВҖВҷs what they are, itГўВҖВҷs what they do, and boy do they do it well. But lest we forget that the whole is greater than the sum of its parts, or that the parts extend far beyond the confines of athleticism alone. These redemption narratives speak to their achievements, but they also function to challenge (if not altogether reframe) the notion of what it means to be a great athlete ГўВҖВ” before they were Olympians, they were Simone, Sunisa and ShaГўВҖВҷCarri.ГӮ

This knowledge also allows us laypeople to find some comfort in compelling Olympic comeback tales. Perhaps the most honest yet hard-to-grapple-with truth about Olympians is that theyГўВҖВҷre quite like us ГўВҖВ” their inspiring stories serve as reminders of our own capabilities (albeit less coordinated ones), goading us to take the rarely invested time to reflect inward on what weГўВҖВҷve accomplished in our own lives. Perhaps, they might even encourage us to hold our hearts and minds in kinder regard, refuting societyГўВҖВҷs prevailing disregard for mental health.ГӮ

After winning the 100-meter dash final at the Stade de France, U.S. sprinterNoah Lylespublished a candidtweet:ГўВҖВңI have Asthma, allergies, dyslexia, ADD, anxiety, and Depression. But I will tell you that what you have does not define what you can become. Why Not You!ГўВҖВқГӮ

LylesГўВҖВҷ message quite cloudlessly encapsulates the crux of the multifaceted elements of an athlete, of a human being ГўВҖВ” to become theworldГўВҖВҷs fastest man,he had to nurtureallof his needs, not simply fine-tune his physicality.ГӮ

His remarks are also a steady reminder of the way disability, despite being an ostensible impediment, can be worked with while being exceptionally athletic. When the Olympics end this Sunday, the Paralympics will follow, commencing in Paris on Aug. 28. And if thereГўВҖВҷs something we can all take from watching para-Olympians compete, itГўВҖВҷs that champions are made, not by pandering to the noise, but by believing in the fullest version of oneself.ГӮ

title: ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВҷm not back. IГўВҖВҷm betterГўВҖВқ: What this year's Olympic comebacks tell us - Salon.com
author: Gabriella Ferrigine
description: What this year's Olympic comebacks tell us
